title: EvidenceKM: Survival Analysis of Statistical Significance Across Trust Thresholds in 888 Cochrane Meta-Analyses

sentences.text: Statistical significance in meta-analyses is routinely accepted at face value, yet few tools evaluate how robustly it survives scrutiny across escalating evidence-quality thresholds.

We applied Kaplan-Meier survival analysis to 888 Cochrane meta-analyses (p < 0.05), treating each MA's composite trust score as its event time and measuring 'death' (loss of significance) as any threshold exceeding that score.

A trust-adjusted z-score (z_trust = z_orig × √(score/100)) was computed for each MA; log-rank chi-squared compared survival across the top five clinical domains; simplified Cox proportional hazards quantified the role of five trust components.

Median survival threshold was 65 (50% of MAs would lose significance at trust score 65), and 31.8% (n = 282) were already weakened (z_trust < 1.96) at their own score level; clinical domains differed significantly (log-rank chi-squared = 19.99, df = 4, p = 0.0005).
Robustness score (HR = 0.16) and stability score (HR = 0.17) were the strongest protective components; audit score (HR = 0.41) was intermediate; power score (HR = 0.90, p = 0.11) was non-significant.

The steep survival drop around threshold 65–75 suggests a critical zone where conventional significance is most vulnerable, identifying domains — particularly Diabetes and Hypertension — with systematically lower evidence resilience.
Survival analysis provides a novel lens for grading the durability of meta-analytic significance, enabling threshold-conditional inference in systematic reviews.

methods.km: S(t) = prod(1 - d_i/n_i); Greenwood variance; log-log 95% CI
methods.log_rank: chi-squared statistic; p from chi2 distribution
methods.cox_ph: simplified: HR = exp(SMD of trust component in early vs late event groups)
